content:
The patient presented to the emergency department with acute onset substernal chest pressure and dyspnea. Initial electrocardiogram demonstrated ST-segment depression in the anterior leads with reciprocal changes. Troponin I was elevated at 2.8 ng/mL, consistent with acute myocardial infarction. Coronary angiography revealed a critical stenosis in the mid-left anterior descending artery with TIMI II flow. The patient underwent successful percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a drug-eluting stent to the LAD with restoration of TIMI III flow and excellent angiographic result. No complications were noted during the procedure.

Post-intervention, the patient was admitted to the coronary care unit for continuous monitoring and medical optimization. Transthoracic echocardiography on hospital day two revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38 percent with anterior wall hypokinesis consistent with the acute infarction territory. B-type natriuretic peptide was mildly elevated at 285 pg/mL. The patient was initiated on guideline-directed medical therapy including aspirin, ticagrelor, high-intensity atorvastatin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. She remained hemodynamically stable throughout the admission with no recurrent angina, arrhythmias, or signs of acute decompensation. Troponin levels peaked at 4.1 ng/mL on hospital day one and trended appropriately downward thereafter.

The patient was discharged on hospital day five in stable condition with appropriate follow-up arranged. She will continue dual antiplatelet therapy for twelve months and has been counseled on medication adherence, dietary modifications, and cardiac rehabilitation participation. Outpatient cardiology follow-up is scheduled in two weeks to reassess left ventricular function and optimize heart failure medications as needed. The patient demonstrated good understanding of discharge instructions and expressed commitment to lifestyle modifications.
